message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Purpose</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    To examine the time-varying reproduction number,
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    , for COVID-19 in Arkansas and Kentucky and investigate the impact of policies and preventative measures on the variability in
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    .
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Arkansas and Kentucky county-level COVID-19 cumulative case count data (March 6-November 7, 2020) were obtained.
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    was estimated using the R package ‘EpiEstim’, by county, region (Delta, non-Delta, Appalachian, non-Appalachian), and policy measures.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    was initially high, falling below 1 in May or June depending on the region, before stabilizing around 1 in the later months. The median
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    for Arkansas and Kentucky at the end of the study were 1.15 (95% credible interval [CrI], 1.13, 1.18) and 1.10 (95% CrI, 1.08, 1.12), respectively, and remained above 1 for the non-Appalachian region.
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    decreased when facial coverings were mandated, changing by -10.64% (95% CrI, -10.60%, -10.70%) in Arkansas and -5.93% (95% CrI, -4.31%, -7.65%) in Kentucky. The trends in
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    estimates were mostly associated with the implementation and relaxation of social distancing measures.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Arkansas and Kentucky maintained a median
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    above 1 during the entire study period. Changes in
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    estimates allows quantitative estimates of potential impact of policies such as facemask mandate.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Spatially refined time-varying reproduction numbers of SARS-CoV-2 in Arkansas and Kentucky and their relationship to population size and public health policy, March – November, 2020
